{"source": "SOURCE SUMMARY:\n# AgentKit\nURL: /integrations/agentkit\n\n---\ntitle: AgentKit\nsidebarTitle: AgentKit\ndescription: Build multi-agent networks in TypeScript with routing, shared state, and MCP tools.\nllm: true\n---\n\nAgentKit is Inngest's TypeScript framework for building agent networks: single agents or coordinated teams that share state and route work through code or model-driven routers. The Steel integration runs each agent's browser actions on a Steel cloud session, so AgentKit handles the orchestration and Steel handles the browser.\n\nYou can also expose MCP servers as tools and stream tokens and tool steps to a UI as the agent runs.\n\n### Requirements\n\n*   **Steel API Key**: Active Steel subscription\n*   **Node.js**: v20+\n*   **Packages**: `@inngest/agent-kit`, `inngest`\n*   **Model provider key**: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, or any OpenAI-compatible endpoint\n\n### Connect Steel to AgentKit\n\nInside an AgentKit tool handler, create a Steel session and hand a Playwright `page` to your agent:\n\n```typescript Typescript -wc\nimport { chromium } from \"playwright\";\nimport Steel from \"steel-sdk\";\n\nconst steel = new Steel({ steelAPIKey: process.env.STEEL_API_KEY! });\nconst session = await steel.sessions.create({});\nconst browser = await chromium.connectOverCDP(\n  `${session.websocketUrl}&apiKey=${process.env.STEEL_API_KEY}`,\n);\nconst page = browser.contexts()[0].pages()[0];\n// `page` is your Steel browser. Call it from AgentKit tool handlers\n```\n\nFull runnable starter: [Steel + AgentKit recipe \u2192](/cookbook/agentkit)\n\n### FAQ\n\n### Do I need to change my existing AgentKit code to use Steel?\n\nNo \u2014 AgentKit keeps handling orchestration, routing, and shared state. Inside a tool handler you create a Steel session, connect Playwright over CDP, and call the resulting `page` from your handlers.\n\n### How do I connect AgentKit to a Steel browser session?\n\nCall `steel.sessions.create()`, connect with `chromium.connectOverCDP()` using the session's `websocketUrl` plus your `apiKey`, and take the first page of `browser.contexts()[0]` as the page your AgentKit tool handlers drive.\n\n### Does AgentKit work with Steel's proxies, stealth mode, and CAPTCHA solving?\n\nYes \u2014 pass them to `sessions.create()` (e.g. `useProxy`, `solveCaptcha`, `stealthConfig`). They're Steel-side session options, so your agent network and routers don't change.\n\n\n### Resources\n\n*   [AgentKit documentation](https://agentkit.inngest.com/overview) \u2013 Official documentation for AgentKit\n*   [Examples gallery](https://agentkit.inngest.com/examples/overview) \u2013 Starter projects (support agent, SWE-bench, coding agent, web search)\n*   [LLMs docs bundle](https://agentkit.inngest.com/llms-full.txt) \u2013 Markdown doc set for IDEs/LLMs\n*   [Inngest Dev Server](https://agentkit.inngest.com/getting-started/local-development) \u2013 Live traces and I/O logs\n*   [Steel Sessions API reference](/api-reference) \u2013 Programmatic session control\n*   [Steel Discord](https://discord.gg/steel-dev) \u2013 Get help and share what you build\n\n\n# Agno\nURL: /integrations/agno\n\n---\ntitle: Agno\nsidebarTitle: Agno\ndescription: Build Python agent teams with shared memory, reasoning, and RAG.\nllm: true\n---\n\nAgno is a Python framework for building multi-agent systems with shared memory, knowledge, and reasoning. The Steel integration wraps a Steel browser as an Agno toolkit, so Agno agents can navigate, fill forms, extract data, and combine browsing with retrieval-augmented reasoning.\n\nAgno is model-agnostic and natively multi-modal, which pairs well with Steel's reliable, sandboxed cloud browsers.\n\n### Requirements\n\n*   **Steel API Key**: Active Steel subscription\n*   **Model provider key**: OpenAI, Anthropic, or any Agno-supported provider\n*   **Python**: 3.10+\n\n### Connect Steel to Agno\n\nWrap a Steel-backed Playwright `page` in an Agno `Toolkit`:\n\n```python Python -wc\nimport os\nfrom playwright.sync_api import sync_playwright\nfrom steel import Steel\n\nsteel = Steel(steel_api_key=os.environ[\"STEEL_API_KEY\"])\nsession = steel.sessions.create()\n\ncraping to answer browser research questions.\"} topics={['Agents', 'Restate']} languages={['TypeScript', 'Python', 'Rust', 'Go']} date=\"2026-06-29\" />\n<RecipeCard slug=\"mcp\" title={\"Expose a Steel browser to any MCP client\"} description={\"Build a Model Context Protocol server in Go with the official SDK and chromedp that hands any MCP client a Steel cloud browser through explicit session-handle tools.\"} topics={['Agents', 'MCP']} languages={['TypeScript', 'Python', 'Rust', 'Go']} date=\"2026-06-24\" />\n<RecipeCard slug=\"genkit\" title={\"Build a browser agent with Genkit\"} description={\"Use Steel with Genkit Go to build a tool-calling agent that navigates and extracts from a chromedp-backed browser and completes a web task.\"} topics={['Agents']} languages={['Go']} date=\"2026-06-23\" />\n</RecipeGrid>\n\n\n# Build a browser agent with Agno\nURL: /cookbook/agno\n\n---\ntitle: Build a browser agent with Agno\ndescription: Integrate Steel with the Agno agent framework.\n---\n\n<RecipeJsonLd slug=\"agno\" title={\"Build a browser agent with Agno\"} description={\"Integrate Steel with the Agno agent framework.\"} authors={[{\"handle\":\"junhsss\",\"name\":\"Jun Ryu\"}]} datePublished=\"2025-09-05\" dateModified=\"2026-04-24\" sourceUrl=\"https://github.com/steel-dev/steel-cookbook/tree/e86cfbf8ba715cdbbc49fc2ef13e9fd7798695dc/examples/agno\" />\n\n<RecipeMeta href=\"https://github.com/steel-dev/steel-cookbook/tree/e86cfbf8ba715cdbbc49fc2ef13e9fd7798695dc/examples/agno\" path=\"examples/agno\" authors={[{\"handle\":\"junhsss\",\"name\":\"Jun Ryu\",\"avatar\":\"https://github.com/junhsss.png?size=40\"}]} updated=\"2026-04-24\" />\n\n<RecipeQuickstart slug=\"agno\" />\n\nAgno's primitive is `Agent(model=..., tools=[...])`. Tools are typed Python methods grouped into a `Toolkit` subclass; docstrings and type hints become the JSON schema the model sees.\n\nThis recipe wraps a Steel browser in a `SteelTools` toolkit, hands it to an Agent, and lets the model drive the session by picking which method to call and filling in arguments on its own.\n\n```python\ntools = SteelTools(api_key=STEEL_API_KEY)\nagent = Agent(\n    name=\"Web Scraper\",\n    model=OpenAIChat(id=\"gpt-5-nano\", api_key=OPENAI_API_KEY),\n    tools=[tools],\n    instructions=[\n        \"Extract content clearly and format nicely\",\n        \"Always close sessions when done\",\n    ],\n    markdown=True,\n)\n\nresponse = agent.run(TASK)\n```\n\n`SteelTools` subclasses `agno.tools.Toolkit` and registers four bound methods (`navigate_to`, `screenshot`, `get_page_content`, `close_session`). `_ensure_session` and `_initialize_browser` defer the Steel session and Playwright connection until the first tool call, so a task that doesn't need a browser never creates one.\n\n## Run it\n\n```bash\ncd examples/agno\ncp .env.example .env          # set STEEL_API_KEY and OPENAI_API_KEY\nuv run main.py\n```\n\nGet keys from [app.steel.dev](https://app.steel.dev/settings/api-keys) and [platform.openai.com](https://platform.openai.com/api-keys). The default task scrapes [quotes.toscrape.com](https://quotes.toscrape.com) across two pages.\n\nYour output varies. Structure looks like this:\n\n```text\nSteel + Agno Starter\n============================================================\n\nResults:\n\n**Page 1:**\n1. \"The world as we have created it is a process of our thinking...\" - Albert Einstein\n2. \"It is our choices, Harry, that show what we truly are...\" - J.K. Rowling\n3. \"There are only two ways to live your life...\" - Albert Einstein\n\n**Page 2:**\n4. \"This life is what you make it...\" - Marilyn Monroe\n5. \"It takes courage to grow up and become who you really are.\" - E.E. Cummings\n\nDone!\n```\n\nThe `finally` block in `main()` calls `tools.close_session()`, which releases the session even when the agent crashes mid-run.\n\n## Make it yours\n\n- **Change the task.** Set `TASK` in `.env` or edit the default in `main.py`.\n- **Add tools.** Append any method to the `tools` list in `SteelTools.__init__`: `click_selector(selector)`, `fill_form(field, value)`, `wait_for_text(text)`. Typed signature plus docstring,\n\nuct to read `action`, `coordinate`, `text`, and the rest. The same `Input` value goes straight back into `NewBetaToolUseBlock` when echoing the assistant turn, so you never reconstruct it field by field.\n\nScreenshots return to Claude as a `tool_result` whose content is a base64 image, built in `screenshotResult`. The `anthropic-sdk-go` ships `NewBetaToolResultBlock` for text results, but an image result needs the explicit struct: a `BetaToolResultBlockParam` whose `Content` holds a `BetaImageBlockParam` with a `BetaBase64ImageSourceParam`. The `ToolUseID` ties the screenshot back to the call that produced it.\n\n## The loop\n\n`executeTask` seeds the history with the system prompt and the task, then on each turn calls the Beta Messages API and processes the response:\n\n```go\nresp, err := a.anthropicClient.Beta.Messages.New(ctx, anthropic.BetaMessageNewParams{\n    Model:     anthropic.ModelClaudeOpus4_7,\n    MaxTokens: 4096,\n    Messages:  a.messages,\n    Tools:     a.tools,\n    Betas:     []string{\"computer-use-2025-11-24\"},\n})\n```\n\nThe tool is declared once in `NewAgent` with `anthropic.BetaToolUnionParamOfComputerUseTool20251124(viewportHeight, viewportWidth)`, which builds the `computer_20251124` definition. Keep the 1280x768 viewport in sync with the Steel session's `Dimensions` or clicks land in the wrong place. Three conditions end the loop: Claude returns only text (task done), the last assistant messages overlap more than 80% by word content (`wordOverlap`, a cheap stall detector), or the iteration count hits `maxIterations` (50).\n\nOne SDK note worth its own line: `anthropic-sdk-go` v1.51.1 has no named constant for the `computer-use-2025-11-24` beta yet (its newest is `computer-use-2025-01-24`). Because `AnthropicBeta` is a string alias, the raw string in `Betas` is correct and type-checks. Swap in the constant if a later SDK release adds one.\n\n## Run it\n\n```bash\ncd examples/claude-computer-use-go\ncp .env.example .env          # set STEEL_API_KEY and ANTHROPIC_API_KEY\ngo run .\n```\n\nGet keys from [app.steel.dev](https://app.steel.dev/settings/api-keys) and [console.anthropic.com](https://console.anthropic.com/). The default task lives in `.env` as `TASK`; override it per run:\n\n```bash\nTASK=\"Find the current weather in New York City\" go run .\n```\n\nYour output varies. Structure looks like this:\n\n```text\nSteel Session created successfully!\nView live session at: https://app.steel.dev/sessions/ab12cd34...\n\nExecuting task: Go to Steel.dev and find the latest news\n============================================================\nI'll navigate to Steel.dev and look for the latest news.\ncomputer({\"action\":\"key\",\"text\":\"ctrl+l\"})\ncomputer({\"action\":\"type\",\"text\":\"https://steel.dev\"})\ncomputer({\"action\":\"key\",\"text\":\"Return\"})\ncomputer({\"action\":\"screenshot\"})\n...\nTask complete - no further actions requested\n\n============================================================\nTASK EXECUTION COMPLETED\nDuration: 78.4 seconds\nReleasing Steel session...\n```\n\nExpect roughly 60 to 180 seconds and 10 to 40 loop iterations for a simple browsing task. A run costs a few cents of browser time plus the Anthropic tokens for each screenshot. Steel bills per session-minute, so the `defer agent.cleanup(ctx)` in `main` that releases the session is not optional: skip it and the browser runs until the 900000 ms timeout set in `initialize`.\n\n## Make it yours\n\n- **Change the task.** Edit `TASK` in `.env` or pass it inline.\n- **Tune the viewport.** `viewportWidth` and `viewportHeight` set both the Steel `Dimensions` and the tool's `display_*_px`. Keep them equal.\n- **Rework the system prompt.** `browserSystemPrompt` is where the browsing conventions live: date injection, the screenshot-after-submit rule, black-screen recovery.\n- **Raise the ceiling.** `maxIterations` is the safety net for long tasks.\n- **Hand off auth.** Pass `SessionContext` to `Sessions.Create` to start authenticated. See [credentials](/cookbook/credentials) and [auth-context](/cookbook/auth-context\n\nwhole recipe hinges on keeping that distinction straight.\n\n## Wiring a remote file into a DOM input\n\n`page.setInputFiles(\"./local.csv\")` resolves paths on the machine running Playwright. Since Chromium lives on a Steel VM, you need to resolve the path there instead. The `main` function drops down to raw CDP:\n\n```typescript\nconst cdpSession = await currentContext.newCDPSession(page);\nconst document = await cdpSession.send(\"DOM.getDocument\");\n\nconst inputNode = await cdpSession.send(\"DOM.querySelector\", {\n  nodeId: document.root.nodeId,\n  selector: \"#load-file\",\n});\n\nawait cdpSession.send(\"DOM.setFileInputFiles\", {\n  files: [uploadedFile.path],\n  nodeId: inputNode.nodeId,\n});\n```\n\n`DOM.setFileInputFiles` runs browser-side, so `uploadedFile.path` resolves against the session VM, which is exactly where `upload()` wrote the bytes. After that, it's plain Playwright: wait for `svg.main-svg`, scroll into view, screenshot to `stock.png` on your local disk.\n\n## Run it\n\n```bash\ncd examples/files-ts\ncp .env.example .env          # set STEEL_API_KEY\nnpm install\nnpm start\n```\n\nGet a key at [app.steel.dev/settings/api-keys](https://app.steel.dev/settings/api-keys). The script prints a session viewer URL as it starts. Open it in another tab to watch the upload land and the chart render.\n\nYour output varies. Structure looks like this:\n\n```text\nSteel + Files API Starter\n============================================================\n\nCreating Steel session...\nSteel Session created!\nView session at https://app.steel.dev/sessions/ab12cd34...\n\nUploading CSV file to the Steel session...\nCSV file uploaded successfully!\nFile path on Steel session: stock.csv\n\nConnected to browser via Playwright\n\nReleasing session...\nSession released\nDone!\n```\n\n`stock.png` lands in the recipe folder. It's the rendered chart, captured server-side after the CSV was parsed remotely, then saved locally. A run takes ~15 seconds.\n\n## The rest of the surface\n\nThe recipe touches `upload` and nothing else, but `client.sessions.files` has more:\n\n- `list(sessionId)`: returns every file in the session namespace with `{ path, size, lastModified }`. Useful after the browser triggers a download and you need to find the new file.\n- `download(sessionId, path)`: pulls a single file back out. Stream the response body to disk.\n- `downloadArchive(sessionId)`: zips the whole namespace into one response. One call instead of N.\n- `delete(sessionId, path)` and `deleteAll(sessionId)`: explicit cleanup. Releasing the session also clears storage.\n\nBrowser-initiated downloads (PDF exports, file-save dialogs) land in the same namespace automatically, so the inverse of this recipe is: drive the page to export, then `list()` and `download()` what showed up.\n\nThere's also `client.files` (without `.sessions`), an organization-scoped store that persists across sessions. Same method shape. Useful for fixtures and assets you don't want to re-upload every run.\n\n## Make it yours\n\n- **Upload from a URL.** Pass a string instead of a `File`: `client.sessions.files.upload(session.id, { file: \"https://example.com/report.pdf\" })`. Steel fetches it server-side and drops it in the session namespace, skipping your machine entirely.\n- **Harvest generated files.** Swap the `csvplot.com` flow for a site that exports. After the download fires, call `list()` to discover the new path, then `download()` it back.\n- **Target a nested path.** `upload()` accepts a `path` argument to control where the file lands inside the sandbox. Default is the filename at root; pass `path: \"inputs/stock.csv\"` to nest.\n\n## Related\n\n[Credentials](/cookbook/credentials) for auth tokens kept out of the filesystem. [Auth context](/cookbook/auth-context) for cookies and storage state. [Profiles](/cookbook/profiles) for persistent user-data directories across runs. [Extensions](/cookbook/extensions) for loading unpacked Chrome extensions into a session.\n\n</Tab>\n\n<Tab id=\"python\" className=\"cookbook-concept-tab\">\n\n<RecipeMeta href=\"https://github.com/s\n\nn Markdown. Input: one absolute URL.\" }\n\nfunc (t scrapeTool) Call(ctx context.Context, input string) (string, error) {\n    url := strings.Trim(strings.TrimSpace(input), \"\\\"'\")\n    resp, err := t.client.Scrape(ctx, steel.ClientScrapeParams{\n        URL:    url,\n        Format: &[]steel.ScrapeRequestFormatItem{steel.ScrapeRequestFormatItemMarkdown},\n    })\n    // ... return the capped resp.Content.Markdown\n}\n```\n\nThe input arrives as a plain string because a ReAct agent emits `Action: scrape` then `Action Input: https://...` as text, and the executor hands you whatever follows. That is why `Call` trims surrounding quotes and whitespace before using the URL: the model's formatting is not guaranteed. There is no JSON schema and no typed argument struct, which is the trade LangChainGo makes for running on any text model.\n\nWiring the agent is one call:\n\n```go\nexecutor, err := agents.Initialize(\n    llm,\n    []tools.Tool{scrapeTool{client: client}},\n    agents.ZeroShotReactDescription,\n    agents.WithMaxIterations(5),\n)\nanswer, err := chains.Run(ctx, executor, task)\n```\n\n`Initialize` builds the MRKL agent and wraps it in an `Executor`, which is itself a chain, so `chains.Run` drives the whole reason-act loop and returns the final string. `WithMaxIterations(5)` caps the loop so a model that never emits `Final Answer:` cannot spin forever.\n\n## Run it\n\n```bash\ncd examples/langchaingo\ncp .env.example .env          # set STEEL_API_KEY and ANTHROPIC_API_KEY\ngo run .\n```\n\nGet a Steel key at [app.steel.dev/settings/api-keys](https://app.steel.dev/settings/api-keys) and an Anthropic key at [console.anthropic.com](https://console.anthropic.com/settings/keys). Your output varies. Structure looks like this:\n\n```text\nRunning LangChainGo agent...\n\nThe top 3 Hacker News stories right now are:\n1. \"...\" with 512 points\n2. \"...\" with 488 points\n3. \"...\" with 401 points\n```\n\nEach scrape call spins up a short-lived Steel browser server-side, so a run costs a few cents of browser time plus the Anthropic tokens for the ReAct loop. There is no session to release: `scrape` opens and closes its own browser per call.\n\n## Make it yours\n\n- **Swap the task.** Change `task` in `main.go`. The tool stays the same; the agent re-plans against the new goal.\n- **Add a tool.** Any struct with `Name`, `Description`, and `Call` slots into the `[]tools.Tool` list. A second tool backed by `client.Screenshot`, or one of LangChainGo's built-ins like the calculator, drops straight in and the MRKL agent picks per step.\n- **Change the model.** Pass a different id to `anthropic.WithModel`, or swap `anthropic.New` for `openai.New` (LangChainGo ships both). The tool is unaffected.\n- **Use native tool-calling.** `agents.NewOpenAIFunctionsAgent` replaces ReAct text parsing with structured function calls on models that support them.\n\n## Related\n\n[eino](/cookbook/eino) is the closest sibling: another Go ReAct agent on Steel's scrape API, but with typed tool arguments instead of LangChainGo's string interface. [genkit](/cookbook/genkit) drives a chromedp browser instead of the scrape endpoint. The [LangChainGo docs](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/tmc/langchaingo) cover chains, memory, and the agent types.\n\n## Related recipes\n\n<RecipeGrid>\n<RecipeCard slug=\"restate-agent\" title={\"Run a durable browser agent with Restate\"} description={\"Build a Restate Virtual Object in TypeScript that uses durable OpenAI planning steps and Steel scraping to answer browser research questions.\"} topics={['Agents', 'Restate']} languages={['TypeScript', 'Python', 'Rust', 'Go']} date=\"2026-06-29\" />\n<RecipeCard slug=\"mcp\" title={\"Expose a Steel browser to any MCP client\"} description={\"Build a Model Context Protocol server in Go with the official SDK and chromedp that hands any MCP client a Steel cloud browser through explicit session-handle tools.\"} topics={['Agents', 'MCP']} languages={['TypeScript', 'Python', 'Rust', 'Go']} date=\"2026-06-24\" />\n<RecipeCard slug=\"genkit\" title={\"Build a browser agent with Ge\n\ncript and Python bindings expose.\n\n## The driver, not the browser\n\nplaywright-go is not a pure-Go CDP client the way [chromedp](/cookbook/chromedp) and [Rod](/cookbook/rod) are. It drives the same Node-based Playwright driver the other language bindings use, so that driver has to exist on disk before `playwright.Run()` will start. The program installs it on the first line of `run`:\n\n```go\nif err := playwright.Install(&playwright.RunOptions{SkipInstallBrowsers: true}); err != nil {\n    return fmt.Errorf(\"install driver: %w\", err)\n}\npw, err := playwright.Run()\n```\n\n`SkipInstallBrowsers: true` is the part that matters for Steel. A normal Playwright install also downloads Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit, hundreds of megabytes you never run, because the browser lives in Steel's cloud, not on your machine. The flag fetches the driver alone. Drop it and the first run still works, but it pulls three browser engines you will never launch. Calling `Install` in code is convenient for a one-file example; in a larger project you would run `go run github.com/playwright-community/playwright-go/cmd/playwright install` once at build time instead and let `Run` assume the driver is present.\n\nThe extraction reads the way it does in [chromedp](/cookbook/chromedp) for the same reason. `page.Evaluate` returns an `interface{}`, and a slice of structs does not cross that boundary cleanly, so the page-side script `JSON.stringify`s its result and the Go side `json.Unmarshal`s the string into a typed `[]story`. The `extractTopStories` constant holds that script: it pulls title, link, and points from the top five `tr.athing` rows.\n\n## Run it\n\n```bash\ncd examples/playwright-go\ncp .env.example .env          # set STEEL_API_KEY\ngo mod tidy\ngo run .\n```\n\nGet a key at [app.steel.dev/settings/api-keys](https://app.steel.dev/settings/api-keys). The first `go run` downloads the Playwright driver, which takes a moment; later runs reuse it. The program prints a session viewer URL as it starts. Open it in another tab to watch the page load live, and it writes `hackernews.png` to the working directory on the way out.\n\nYour output varies with the site. Structure looks like this:\n\n```text\nCreating Steel session...\nSession created. Watch it live at https://app.steel.dev/sessions/ab12cd34\nConnected to browser via playwright-go\nNavigating to Hacker News...\n\nTop 5 Hacker News Stories:\n\n1. A compiler that fits in a tweet\n   Link: https://example.com/tiny-compiler\n   Points: 521\n\n2. Show HN: I mapped every CDP command to a Go method\n   Link: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=43990011\n   Points: 274\n\nSaved screenshot to hackernews.png\nReleasing session...\n```\n\nA run costs a few cents of browser time. Steel bills per session-minute, so the deferred `client.Sessions.Release` is not optional. The `defer` sits right after the create call, so the session is released whether `run` returns clean or errors partway through. Drop it and the browser stays up until the default five-minute timeout, on your dime.\n\n## Make it yours\n\n- **Swap the target.** Change the `page.Goto` URL and the `extractTopStories` script. The JSON-string bridge works for any shape: define a matching Go struct and unmarshal. Session setup and cleanup stay identical.\n- **Skip the JS.** `page.QuerySelectorAll(\"tr.athing\")` returns `ElementHandle` values with `TextContent` and `GetAttribute`, if you would rather query node by node than evaluate a script. It is more round-trips and easier to debug one selector at a time.\n- **Turn on stealth.** `SessionCreateParams` carries `UseProxy`, `SolveCaptcha`, and `Timeout` for sites with anti-bot defenses. Set them on the struct you pass to `Sessions.Create`.\n- **Add steps.** Playwright auto-waits on actionability, so `page.Click`, `page.Fill`, and `page.WaitForSelector` are reliable without manual sleeps when you fill forms or paginate before extracting.\n\n## Related\n\n[chromedp](/cookbook/chromedp) and [Rod](/cookbook/rod) are the pure-Go options: both speak CDP directly wit\n\n-29\" />\n<RecipeCard slug=\"headless-chrome\" title={\"Automate a cloud browser with headless_chrome\"} description={\"Use Steel with headless_chrome, the synchronous Rust equivalent of Puppeteer, to connect over CDP and scrape quotes with element handles.\"} topics={['Browser automation']} languages={['Rust']} date=\"2026-06-24\" />\n</RecipeGrid>\n\n\n# Automate browsing with natural-language instructions using Stagehand\nURL: /cookbook/stagehand\n\n---\ntitle: Automate browsing with natural-language instructions using Stagehand\ndescription: Use Steel with Stagehand for natural-language-driven AI browser automation.\n---\n\n<RecipeJsonLd slug=\"stagehand\" title={\"Automate browsing with natural-language instructions using Stagehand\"} description={\"Use Steel with Stagehand for natural-language-driven AI browser automation.\"} authors={[{\"handle\":\"junhsss\",\"name\":\"Jun Ryu\"}]} datePublished=\"2025-07-16\" dateModified=\"2026-04-24\" sourceUrl=\"https://github.com/steel-dev/steel-cookbook/tree/e86cfbf8ba715cdbbc49fc2ef13e9fd7798695dc/examples/stagehand-ts\" />\n\n<Tabs items={['TypeScript', 'Python']} groupId=\"lang\" persist updateAnchor className=\"cookbook-concept-tabs\">\n\n<Tab id=\"typescript\" className=\"cookbook-concept-tab\">\n\n<RecipeMeta href=\"https://github.com/steel-dev/steel-cookbook/tree/e86cfbf8ba715cdbbc49fc2ef13e9fd7798695dc/examples/stagehand-ts\" path=\"examples/stagehand-ts\" authors={[{\"handle\":\"junhsss\",\"name\":\"Jun Ryu\",\"avatar\":\"https://github.com/junhsss.png?size=40\"}]} updated=\"2026-04-24\" />\n\n<RecipeQuickstart slug=\"stagehand-ts\" />\n\nStagehand replaces brittle selectors with two LLM-backed primitives:\n\n- `stagehand.extract(instruction, schema)`: describe what you want, pass a Zod schema, get typed data back.\n- `stagehand.act(instruction)`: describe an action in natural language, Stagehand figures out the click / type / scroll.\n\nBoth run against a Steel session over CDP, so Stagehand handles the reasoning and Steel handles the browser (stealth, proxies, live viewer).\n\n```typescript\nstagehand = new Stagehand({\n  env: \"LOCAL\",\n  localBrowserLaunchOptions: {\n    cdpUrl: `${session.websocketUrl}&apiKey=${STEEL_API_KEY}`,\n  },\n  model: { modelName: \"openai/gpt-5\", apiKey: OPENAI_API_KEY },\n});\n\nawait stagehand.init();\n```\n\n`env: \"LOCAL\"` tells Stagehand \"I'll hand you the browser.\" That browser is Steel, reached via the CDP URL. `model` is the LLM that interprets every instruction. This starter targets **Stagehand v3**.\n\nTyped extraction, an instruction paired with a Zod schema:\n\n```typescript\nconst stories = await stagehand.extract(\n  \"extract the titles and ranks of the first 5 stories on the page\",\n  z.object({\n    stories: z.array(z.object({ title: z.string(), rank: z.number() })),\n  }),\n);\n```\n\nThe schema isn't just documentation. Stagehand constrains the LLM's output against it and gives you a typed result at runtime. Swap the prompt and schema for any extraction problem: forms, tables, search results, prices.\n\nNatural-language action, no selector required:\n\n```typescript\nawait stagehand.act(\"click the 'new' link in the top navigation\");\n```\n\nStagehand inspects the DOM, picks the matching element, and clicks it.\n\n## Run it\n\n```bash\ncd examples/stagehand-ts\ncp .env.example .env          # set STEEL_API_KEY and OPENAI_API_KEY\nnpm install\nnpm start\n```\n\nGet keys from [app.steel.dev](https://app.steel.dev/settings/api-keys) and [platform.openai.com](https://platform.openai.com/api-keys). A session viewer URL prints as the script starts. Open it in another tab to watch Stagehand work.\n\nYour output varies. Structure looks like this:\n\n```text\nCreating Steel session...\nSteel Session created!\nView session at https://app.steel.dev/sessions/ab12cd34\u2026\n\nInitializing Stagehand...\nConnected to browser via Stagehand\nNavigating to Hacker News...\nExtracting top stories using AI...\n\nTop 5 Hacker News Stories:\n1. Claude 4.7 Opus released today\n2. Show HN: A browser extension for reading on slow connections\n3. \u2026\n\nNavigating to HN's 'new' section via a natural-langua\n\nthon', 'Rust', 'Go']} date=\"2026-06-23\" />\n<RecipeCard slug=\"genkit\" title={\"Build a browser agent with Genkit\"} description={\"Use Steel with Genkit Go to build a tool-calling agent that navigates and extracts from a chromedp-backed browser and completes a web task.\"} topics={['Agents']} languages={['Go']} date=\"2026-06-23\" />\n<RecipeCard slug=\"eino\" title={\"Build a browser agent with Eino\"} description={\"Use Steel with the ByteDance Eino framework to build a ReAct agent that calls Steel's scrape API as a tool to research and answer a web question.\"} topics={['Agents']} languages={['Go']} date=\"2026-06-23\" />\n<RecipeCard slug=\"rig\" title={\"Build a browser agent with rig\"} description={\"Use Steel with rig to build an agent that drives a cloud browser over CDP with chromiumoxide through navigate and extract tools, then answers a multi-step web task.\"} topics={['Agents']} languages={['Rust']} date=\"2026-06-23\" />\n<RecipeCard slug=\"swiftide\" title={\"Build a research agent with Swiftide\"} description={\"Use Steel with Swiftide to build an agent whose tool reads the web through Steel's scrape endpoint, so the model works from clean Markdown with no browser library.\"} topics={['Agents']} languages={['Rust']} date=\"2026-06-23\" />\n<RecipeCard slug=\"langchaingo\" title={\"Build a browser agent with LangChainGo\"} description={\"Use Steel with LangChainGo's zero-shot ReAct (MRKL) agent and a string-in, string-out scrape tool so Claude reads a page and answers a question.\"} topics={['Agents']} languages={['Go']} date=\"2026-06-23\" />\n<RecipeCard slug=\"google-adk\" title={\"Build a browser agent with Google ADK\"} description={\"Use Steel with Google's Agent Development Kit (ADK) for Go to build a tool-using browser agent that drives a chromedp session over CDP and reads Hacker News.\"} topics={['Agents', 'Typed output']} languages={['TypeScript', 'Python', 'Go']} date=\"2026-06-23\" />\n<RecipeCard slug=\"microsoft-agent-framework\" title={\"Build a browser agent with Microsoft Agent Framework\"} description={\"Use Steel with Microsoft Agent Framework 1.0 (the successor to AutoGen and Semantic Kernel) to build a tool-using browser agent.\"} topics={['Agents']} languages={['Python']} date=\"2026-05-11\" />\n<RecipeCard slug=\"deep-research\" title={\"Deep research with Claude Agent SDK subagents\"} description={\"Lead orchestrator dispatches parallel researcher subagents, each driving its own Steel browser, and synthesizes findings into a cited Markdown report.\"} topics={['Agents', 'Subagents']} languages={['TypeScript', 'Python']} date=\"2026-05-01\" />\n<RecipeCard slug=\"claude-agent-sdk\" title={\"Build a browser agent with the Claude Agent SDK\"} description={\"Use Steel with the Claude Agent SDK (TypeScript) to build a tool-using browser agent on Anthropic's first-party agent loop.\"} topics={['Agents']} languages={['TypeScript', 'Python']} date=\"2026-04-29\" />\n<RecipeCard slug=\"pydantic-ai\" title={\"Build a typed browser agent with Pydantic AI\"} description={\"Use Steel with Pydantic AI to build typed, provider-agnostic browser agents with dependency injection.\"} topics={['Agents', 'Typed output']} languages={['Python']} date=\"2026-04-29\" />\n<RecipeCard slug=\"langgraph\" title={\"Build a typed browser agent with LangGraph\"} description={\"Use Steel with LangGraph to build a typed browser agent with an explicit state-machine loop and a structured-output formatter node.\"} topics={['Agents', 'Typed output']} languages={['Python']} date=\"2026-04-29\" />\n<RecipeCard slug=\"mastra\" title={\"Build a typed browser agent with Mastra\"} description={\"Use Steel with Mastra to build a typed browser agent with the Mastra Model Router and Studio playground.\"} topics={['Agents', 'Typed output']} languages={['TypeScript']} date=\"2026-04-29\" />\n<RecipeCard slug=\"openai-agents\" title={\"Build a typed browser agent with the OpenAI Agents SDK\"} description={\"Use Steel with the OpenAI Agents SDK for TypeScript to build typed, tool-using browser agents.\"} topics={['Agents', 'Typed output']} languages\n\nsimple/smaller file uploads,\n// using standard automation library methods will look at local files\nawait page.setInputFiles(\"#file-input\", [uploadedSessionFile.path]);\n```\n\n\n#### Browser-Use Example\n\nBrowser-use needs some setup before it can be used. This includes setting up the browser profile with the correct downloads path and adding in a step hook to extract downloaded files to your local machine if necessary.\n\n```python Python -wcn -f main.py\n# Before agent main loop...\n\n# Hook to extract downloaded files to local machine if necessary\nasync def step_hook_start(agent):\n    if os.environ.get(\"BROWSER_PROVIDER\") == \"steel\":\n        await agent._check_and_update_downloads()\n        if agent.available_file_paths and len(agent.available_file_paths) > 0:\n            has_new_files = False\n            for file_path in agent.available_file_paths:\n                if file_path not in downloaded_files:\n                    downloaded_files.append(file_path)\n                    has_new_files = True\n            if has_new_files:\n                try:\n                    extracted_files = await browser_service.extract_downloaded_files(DOWNLOAD_PATH)\n                    logger.info(f\"Extracted files: {extracted_files}\")\n                except Exception as e:\n                    logger.error(f\"Failed to extract downloaded files: {e}\")\n\nasync def main():\n    try:\n        browser_session = Browser(cdp_url=cdp_url, downloads_path=\"/files\")\n        await browser_session.connect()\n        await browser_session.cdp_client.send.Target.createBrowserContext()\n        browser_context_ids_return = await browser_session.cdp_client.send.Target.getBrowserContexts()\n        browser_context_ids = browser_context_ids_return['browserContextIds']\n        browser_context_id = browser_context_ids[0]\n        await browser_session.cdp_client.send.Browser.setDownloadBehavior(params={\"behavior\": \"allow\", \"downloadPath\": \"/files\", \"eventsEnabled\": True, \"browserContextId\": browser_context_id})\n        agent = Agent(task=TASK, llm=model, browser_session=browser_session)\n        agent.browser_session.browser_profile.downloads_path = LOCAL_DOWNLOAD_PATH\n        agent_results = await agent.run(\n            on_step_start=step_hook_start,\n            max_steps=5\n        )\n    except Exception as e:\n        print(f\"Error: {e}\")\n    finally:\n        # Clean up resources\n        if session:\n            client.sessions.release(session.id)\n            print(\"Session released\")\n        print(\"Done!\")\n# Rest of code...\n```\n\n\n#### Complete Example\n\nEnd-to-end workflow demonstrating global file management and session file operations.\n\n```typescript Typescript -wcn -f main.ts\nimport dotenv from \"dotenv\";\nimport fs from \"fs\";\nimport { chromium } from \"playwright\";\nimport Steel from \"steel-sdk\";\n\ndotenv.config();\n\nconst client = new Steel({\n  steelAPIKey: process.env.STEEL_API_KEY,\n});\n\nasync function main() {\n  let session;\n  let browser;\n\n  try {\n    // Upload dataset to global storage for reuse\n    const datasetFile = new File(\n      [fs.readFileSync(\"./data/stock-data.csv\")],\n      \"stock-data.csv\",\n      { type: \"text/csv\" }\n    );\n\n    const globalFile = await client.files.upload({ file: datasetFile });\n    console.log(`Dataset uploaded to global storage: ${globalFile.path}`);\n\n    // Create session and mount global file\n    session = await client.sessions.create();\n    console.log(`Session created: ${session.sessionViewerUrl}`);\n\n    const sessionFile = await client.sessions.files.upload(session.id, {\n      file: globalFile.path\n    });\n\n    // Connect browser and use the file\n    browser = await chromium.connectOverCDP(\n      `wss://connect.steel.dev?apiKey=${process.env.STEEL_API_KEY}&sessionId=${session.id}`\n    );\n\n    const currentContext = browser.contexts()[0];\n    const page = currentContext.pages()[0];\n\n    // Navigate to data visualization tool\n    await page.goto('https://www.csvplot.com/');\n\n    // Upload file to web application using CDP\n    const cdpSession = awa\n\nive=false`}\n  style=\"width: 100%; height: 600px; border: none;\"\n></iframe>\n```\n\nDisabling interactivity makes the view read-only, ideal for watch-only monitoring scenarios.\n\n---\n\n## Headless (Legacy)\n\n> Headless live sessions remain supported for existing workflows.  \n> They use Chrome\u2019s screencasting instead of WebRTC and expose additional configuration options.\n\n### Configuration Options (Headless Only)\n\n| Parameter | Type | Default | Description |\n|------------|------|----------|-------------|\n| `theme` | string | `\"dark\"` | UI theme (`dark` or `light`) |\n| `interactive` | boolean | `true` | Enable or disable interaction |\n| `showControls` | boolean | `true` | Show or hide navigation UI |\n| `pageId` | string | (empty) | Focus the view on a specific page/tab |\n| `pageIndex` | string | (empty) | Display a specific tab by index |\n\nExample:\n\n```html\n<iframe\n  src={`${session.debugUrl}?theme=light&interactive=true&showControls=true&pageIndex=0`}\n  style=\"width: 100%; height: 600px; border: none;\"\n></iframe>\n```\n\n---\n\n### Common Use Cases\n\n**Read-only viewer**\n\n```html\n<iframe\n  src={`${session.debugUrl}?interactive=false`}\n  style=\"width: 100%; height: 600px; border: none;\"\n></iframe>\n```\n\n**Human-in-the-loop control**\n\nAllow humans to take over automation tasks or debug live workflows interactively using `interactive=true`.\n\n---\n\n### Troubleshooting\n\nIf the embedded view appears blank or unresponsive:\n- Ensure the session is active (default timeout: 5 min).  \n- Confirm your browser supports **H.264 baseline** playback.  \n- Check your container has fixed dimensions (`width` and `height`).  \n- Verify the correct session and valid API key were used.\n\n---\n\n### Summary\n\nAll new sessions now run **headful by default**, streaming real-time video with WebRTC.  \nUse the same `debugUrl` to embed or view \u2014 Steel automatically determines the correct playback mode.  \n\nHeadless live streams remain available for legacy sessions but will be phased out over time.\n\n### FAQ\n\n### Can I embed a live Steel browser session in my app?\n\nYes. Every session created via the API returns a `debugUrl` that you can open directly in a browser or embed in your UI with an iframe, streaming the live browser in real time.\n\n### Can users interact with an embedded live session, or is it view-only?\n\nBoth. The `interactive` parameter (default `true`) enables remote mouse and keyboard input for human-in-the-loop workflows; set `interactive=false` on the debug URL for a read-only, watch-only view.\n\n### Is the debug URL authenticated?\n\nNo. Debug URLs are unauthenticated by design, so anyone with the URL can view or interact with that session. Add your own access controls if you embed live sessions in a user-facing product.\n\n### How does Steel's live view streaming work?\n\nNew sessions run headful by default and stream real-time video over WebRTC at 25 fps using H.264, with OS-level capture and low latency. Legacy headless sessions use the same `debugUrl` but display via Chrome's screencasting, and Steel automatically picks the right playback mode.\n\n\n# Past Sessions\nURL: /overview/sessions-api/embed-sessions/past-sessions\n\n---\ntitle: Past Sessions\nsidebarTitle: Past Sessions\ndescription: How to access recordings of past browser sessions and display them within your app\nllm: true\n---\n\nSteel automatically records every session so you can replay it later.  \nWith the new headful session recordings, you can now embed real MP4 playback \u2014 no event reconstruction, no missing UI elements.\n\nFor older implementations, we still support headless playback via rrweb.\n\n## Embed Headful Session Recordings (Recommended)\n\n### What Changed\nSteel has moved from slow, unreliable screencasting and event-based playback to full OS-level streaming and MP4 recordings.\n\n- 25fps WebRTC-based video streaming  \n- MP4 recordings showing the exact screen output  \n- No discrepancies between actual sessions and replays  \n\n> **Tip:** Headful sessions are now **default** for all Steel sessions.  \n> No changes\n\nSELECTED EVIDENCE CARDS:\n\n[card_006] source extract\nTAGS: source_grounding\nFACTS:\n- -29\" />\n<RecipeCard slug=\"headless-chrome\" title={\"Automate a cloud browser with headless_chrome\"} description={\"Use Steel with headless_chrome, the synchronous Rust equivalent of Puppeteer, to connect over CDP and scrape quotes with element handles.\"} topics={['Browser automation']} languages={['Rust']} date=\"2026-06-24\" primitives:\n\n- `stagehand.extract(instruction, schema)`: describe what you want, pass a Zod schema, get typed data back.\n- `stagehand.act(instruction)`: describe an action in natural language, Stagehand figures out the click / type / scroll.\n\nBoth run against a Steel session over CDP, so Stagehand handles the reasoning and Steel handles the browser (stealth, proxies, live `model` is the LLM that interprets every instruction. This starter targets **Stagehand v3**.\n\nTyped extraction, an instruction paired with a Zod schema:\n\n```typescript\nconst stories = await stagehand.extract(\n  \"extract the titles and ranks of the first 5 stories on the page\",\n  z.object({\n    stories: z.array(z.object({ title: z.string(), rank: z.number() })),\n  }),\n);\n```\n\nThe schema isn't just documentation. Stagehand constrains the LLM's output against it and gives you a typed result at runtime. Swap the prompt and schema for any extraction problem: forms, tables, search results, prices.\n\nNatural-language action, no selector required:\n\n```typescript\nawait stagehand.act(\"click the 'new' link in the top navigation\");\n```\n\nStagehand inspects the DOM, picks the matching element, and clicks it.\n\n## Run it\n\n```bash\ncd examples/stagehand-ts\ncp .env.example .env          # set STEEL_API_KEY and OPENAI_API_KEY\nnpm install\nnpm start\n```\n\nGet keys from [app.steel.dev](https://app.steel.dev/settings/api-keys) and [platform.openai.com](https://platform.openai.com/api-keys). {e}\")\n    finally:\n        # Clean up resources\n        if session:\n            client.sessions.release(session.id)\n            print(\"Session released\")\n        print(\"Done!\")\n# Rest of the view read-only, ideal for watch-only monitoring scenarios.\n\n---\n\n## Headless (Legacy)\n\n> Headless live sessions remain supported for existing workflows.  \n> They use Chrome\u2019s screencasting instead of WebRTC and expose additional configuration options.\n\n### Configuration Options (Headless Only)\n\n| Parameter | Type | Default | Description |\n|------------|------|----------|-------------|\n| `theme` | string | `\"dark\"` | UI theme (`dark` or `light`) |\n| `interactive` | boolean | `true` | Enable or disable interaction |\n| `showControls` | boolean | `true` | Show or hide navigation UI |\n| `pageId` | string | (empty) | Focus the view on a specific page/tab |\n| `pageIndex` | string | (empty) | Display a specific tab by index |\n\nExample:\n\n```html\n<iframe\n  src={`${session.debugUrl}?theme=light&interactive=true&showControls=true&pageIndex=0`}\n  style=\"width: 100%; height: 600px; border: none;\"\n></iframe>\n```\n\n---\n\n### Common Use Cases\n\n**Read-only viewer**\n\n```html\n<iframe\n  src={`${session.debugUrl}?interactive=false`}\n  style=\"width: 100%; height: 600px; border: none;\"\n></iframe>\n```\n\n**Human-in-the-loop control**\n\nAllow humans to take over automation tasks or debug live workflows interactively using `interactive=true`.\n\n---\n\n### Troubleshooting\n\nIf the embedded view appears blank or unresponsive:\n- Ensure the session is active (default timeout: 5 min).  \n- Confirm your browser supports **H.264 baseline** playback.  \n- Check your container has fixed dimensions (`width` and `height`).  \n- Verify the correct session and valid API key were used.\n\n---\n\n### Summary\n\nAll new sessions now run **headful by default**, streaming real-time video with WebRTC.  \nUse the same `debugUrl` to embed or view \u2014 Steel automatically determines the correct playback mode.  \n\nHeadless live streams remain available for legacy sessions but will be phased out over time.\n\n### FAQ\n\n### Can I embed a live Steel browser session in my app?\n\nYes. Every session created via the API returns a `debugUrl` that you can open directly in a browser or embed in your UI with an iframe, streaming the live browser in real time.\n\n### Can users interact with an embedded live session, or is it view-only?\n\nBoth. The `interactive` parameter (default `true`) enables remote mouse and keyboard input for human-in-the-loop workflows; set `interactive=false` on the debug URL for a read-only, watch-only view.\n\n### Is the debug URL authenticated?\n\nNo. Debug URLs are unauthenticated by design, so anyone with the URL can view or interact with that session. Add your own access controls if you embed live sessions in a user-facing product.\n\n### How does Steel's live view streaming work?\n\nNew sessions run headful by default and stream real-time video over WebRTC at 25 fps using H.264, with OS-level capture and low latency. Legacy headless sessions use the same `debugUrl` but display via Chrome's screencasting, and Steel automatically picks the right playback mode.\n\n\n# Past Sessions\nURL: /overview/sessions-api/embed-sessions/past-sessions\n\n---\ntitle: Past Sessions\nsidebarTitle: Past Sessions\ndescription: How to access recordings of past browser sessions and display them within your app\nllm: true\n---\n\nSteel automatically records every session so you can replay it later.  \nWith the new headful session recordings, you can now embed real MP4 playback \u2014 no event reconstruction, no missing UI elements.\n\nFor older implementations, we still support headless playback via rrweb.\n\n## Embed Headful Session Recordings (Recommended)\n\n### What Changed\nSteel has moved from slow, unreliable screencasting and event-based playback to full OS-level streaming and MP4 recordings.\n\n- 25fps WebRTC-based video streaming  \n- MP4 recordings showing the exact screen output  \n- No discrepancies between actual sessions and replays  \n\n> **Tip:** Headful sessions are now **default** for all Steel sessions.  \n> No changes", "example": {"type": "plan synthesis", "skill_tags": ["planning", "causal_reasoning", "tradeoff_analysis"], "context": "You are an engineering assistant supporting a team that builds a customer-facing dashboard embedding remote cloud-browser sessions. Legacy headless sessions use Chrome screencasting and support extra query params: `theme`, `interactive`, `showControls`, `pageId`, `pageIndex`. ~~~~text
# Watch & Take Over Feature: Concrete Implementation Plan

## Executive Summary
I've mapped a workable implementation that bridges current (headful) and legacy (headless) session types, but **three security/reliability gaps must be closed before shipping to support staff**. Below is the detailed plan with config choices and flagged risks.

---

## 1. ARCHITECTURE & MODE TRANSITIONS

### Session State Machine
```
AUTOMATED (read-only monitor)
    ↓ [click "Take Over"]
MANUAL_CONTROL (write access enabled)
    ↓ [click "Return to Automated" OR session times out]
AUTOMATED (read-only again) OR SESSION_ENDED
    ↓
ARCHIVED (post-session review available)
```

### Config: Session Type Detection & Capability Matrix

**At session initialization**, detect and store session metadata:

```json
{
  "sessionId": "abc123",
  "sessionType": "headful" | "legacy_headless",
  "createdAt": "2024-01-15T10:30:00Z",
  "watchModeCapabilities": {
    "headful": {
      "monitorUrl": "{debugUrl}?interactive=false",
      "takeoverUrl": "{debugUrl}?interactive=true",
      "recordingFormat": "mp4_exportable",
      "postSessionReplay": "native_mp4"
    },
    "legacy_headless": {
      "monitorUrl": "{debugUrl}?interactive=false&showControls=false",
      "takeoverUrl": "{debugUrl}?interactive=true&showControls=true",
      "recordingFormat": "rrweb_events",
      "postSessionReplay": "rrweb_reconstruction"
    }
  },
  "inactivityTimeoutMin": 5,
  "isLiveSession": true,
  "archivedAt": null
}
```

---

## 2. IMPLEMENTATION STEPS

### 2.1 Watch Mode (Read-Only Monitoring)

**UI Component: Session Monitor Panel**
- Load appropriate `debugUrl` in iframe based on session type
- **Headful**: Use `{debugUrl}?interactive=false` (WebRTC stream, no input handling)
- **Legacy headless**: Use `{debugUrl}?interactive=false&showControls=false` (screencasting, no controls rendered)
- Display overlay controls: `[Take Over]` button, session status, elapsed time, inactivity warning
- Listen for session timeout event → auto-transition to "SESSION_ENDED"

**Code sketch:**
```javascript
const loadWatchMode = (session) => {
  const monitorUrl = session.watchModeCapabilities[session.sessionType].monitorUrl;
  // Set iframe.src = monitorUrl
  // Disable iframe pointer events: iframe { pointer-events: none; }
  // Listen to sessionTimeoutEvent() → handleSessionEnded()
}
```

### 2.2 Take-Over Transition (Single-Click Handoff)

**On "[Take Over]" button click:**

1. **Capture handoff context**: Record timestamp, support agent ID, reason (optional)
2. **Reload iframe with interactive mode**:
   - Headful: `{debugUrl}?interactive=true` 
   - Legacy: `{debugUrl}?interactive=true&showControls
~~~~

#### Model Response

~~~~text
# Watch & Take-Over Feature: Concrete Implementation Plan

## 1. ARCHITECTURE & MODE TRANSITIONS

### Session State Machine
```
┌─────────────┐
│   RUNNING   │ (automated task in progress)
│  [WATCH]    │ ◄──────────────────────────┐
└──────┬──────┘                            │
       │ [TAKE OVER clicked]               │
       ▼                                   │
┌─────────────────────┐                   │
│ CONTROL_TRANSITION  │ (100ms handoff)    │
│ (pause automation)  │                   │
└──────┬──────────────┘                   │
       │                                  │
       ▼                                  │
┌──────────────────┐     [RELEASE]       │
│ MANUAL_CONTROL   │──────────────────────┘
│ (agent driving)  │                      
└──────────────────┘                      
       │                                  
       ├─ [session timeout] ──► [INACTIVE]
       └─ [task completes] ───► [ENDED]
```

### Implementation Details

**Watch Mode (Passive Monitoring)**
- Load debug URL with `interactive=false` in agent's iframe
- Current headful: WebRTC stream read-only, no input channel
- Legacy headless: rrweb replay with `showControls=false`
- Lightweight—no server-side state change required
- Display: 1-second delayed stream + agent interaction log sidebar

**Take-Over Transition**
```javascript
// Config for both session types
const takeoverConfig = {
  headful: {
    action: 'toggle_interactive_param',

{"user_prompt": "We're adding a 'watch and take over' feature to our internal ops console: support agents should be able to passively monitor an automated browser session, and with one click, switch into full manual control to fix a stuck task. Afterward, we want a way to review what happened on any session, even hours later, without needing the session still running. Give me a concrete plan (embed config choices, mode transitions, and post-session review path) that fits both current and older session types we might still have running, and flag any security or reliability gaps I should close before shipping this to support staff.", "context": "You are an engineering assistant supporting a team that builds a customer-facing dashboard embedding remote cloud-browser sessions. Raw facts available to you from provider documentation: Sessions expose a `debugUrl` usable in an iframe. An `interactive` query param exists on the debug URL. Headful sessions are the current default and stream via WebRTC/H.264 at 25fps with OS-level capture. Legacy headless sessions use Chrome screencasting and support extra query params: `theme`, `interactive`, `showControls`, `pageId`, `pageIndex`. Debug URLs carry no authentication token requirement of their own. Sessions have a default timeout (5 min) after which they go inactive. Every session is recorded automatically; headful recordings are exportable as MP4, legacy sessions replay via rrweb event reconstruction. A separate 'past sessions' endpoint/UI surfaces recordings after a session ends."}
~~~~

#### Model Response

~~~~text
# Watch & Takeover Feature: Concrete Implementation Plan

## 1. ARCHITECTURE & MODE TRANSITIONS

### Session Monitoring States
```
AUTOMATED (default)
  ├─ [Support agent opens console]
  └─> WATCHED (read-only iframe, no interaction)
       ├─ [Agent clicks "Take Control"]
       └─> MANUAL (agent has full input control)
            ├─ [Agent clicks "Release" or timeout]
            └─> AUTOMATED (resumes if task not completed)
                 └─> [Session ends normally]
                      └─> ARCHIVED (review-only state)
```

### Implementation Details

**Monitoring Mode (Read-Only)**
- Load debug URL in iframe: `debugUrl?interactive=false`
- Apply CSS `pointer-events: none` as additional protection layer
- Display UI overlay: "Watching live session" with timestamp + takeover button
- For legacy headless: append `&showControls=false` to suppress conflicting UI elements
- Poll session status every 2 seconds to detect stuck states (no frame updates for >10s = candidate for intervention)

**Takeover Activation**
- Single-click button triggers: `debugUrl?interactive=true` (reload iframe, or re-establish if WebRTC connection)
- Log takeover event with agent ID + timestamp immediately
- Inject visual indicator: subtle border color change or "MANUAL CONTROL ACTIVE" banner
- Disable automatic task completion; any ongoing automation pauses
- **Critical**: Set session timeout extension on takeover (reset 5-min timer; extend to 30 min max to prevent accidental abandonment)

**Release/Handoff**
- Agent clicks "Return to Automation" → iframe reloads with `interactive=false`
- If task logic supports mid-session resume: trigger resume signal to automation layer
- Otherwise: log completion status and end session normally
- Timeout resets to default 5 min

---

## 2. POST-SESSION REVIEW PATH

### Accessing Historical Sessions
```
Timeline View:
  [Past Sessions List (filtered by date/agent/customer)]
    └─ [Click session] → Review Player
         ├─ Recording playback (MP4 for headful, rrweb reconstruction for legacy)
         ├─ Event stream timeline (automated → watched → manual → automated → ended)
         ├─ Agent annotations sidebar (optional: what happened, why takeover needed)
         └─ Inspector: frame-by-frame scrubbing, DOM snapshots at key moments
```

### Data Retention & Playback
- **Headful recordings**: Export MP4 from provider immediately on session end; store with metadata (session ID, agent ID, customer, timestamps of mode switches)
- **Legacy headless recordings**: Archive rrweb event log JSON; replay engine reconstructs session with seek capability
- **Event log structure** (both types):
  ```json
  {
    "sessionId": "...",
    "events": [
      { "ts": 12345, "type": "mode_change", "from": "AUTOMATED", "to": "WATCHED", "agentId": "..." },
      { "ts": 12400, "type": "mode_change", "from": "WATCHED", "to": "MANUAL", "agentId": "..." },
      { "ts": 12800, "type": "mode_change", "from": "MANUAL", "to":
~~~~

~~~~text
# Watch-and-Take-Over Feature: Implementation Plan

## 1. Embed configuration (branch by session type)

You need a runtime check for session type, since the two generations of sessions expose different debug-URL capabilities.

**Headful sessions (current default, WebRTC/H.264, OS-level capture)**
- Embed `debugUrl` in the iframe.
- Toggle `interactive=false` for passive watch mode, `interactive=true` for takeover mode. This is the only mode-relevant param exposed for headful — there's no separate "chrome" you can hide/show, because capture is OS-level (the whole rendered surface streams, not just page content).
- No `showControls`, `pageId`, or `pageIndex` equivalent — treat headful as "what you see is the whole desktop-level view."

**Legacy headless sessions (Chrome screencast, still may be running)**
- Embed `debugUrl` with:
  - `interactive=false` + `showControls=false` for watch mode (clean view, no accidental input, no chrome UI).
  - `interactive=true` + `showControls=true` on takeover (agent gets input control and navigation chrome).
  - `theme` set to match your ops console's light/dark mode for visual consistency.
  - `pageId`/`pageIndex` used to target the correct tab if the automated session has multiple pages open — surface a tab-picker in your UI if `pageIndex` count > 1, since legacy sessions can have multiple tabs and this doesn't apply to headful.

**Config table to drive this in code:**

| Session type | Watch mode params | Takeover mode params |
|---|---|---|
| Headful | `interactive=false` | `interactive=true` |
| Legacy headless | `interactive=false&showControls=false&pageId=<id>` | `interactive=true&showControls=true&pageId=<id>` |

Store session generation as a field on your session record at creation time so the frontend doesn't have to guess — don't rely on parsing the debug URL to infer type.

## 2. Mode transition ("one click" watch → takeover)

- The support UI holds a single "Take Over" button. On click:
  1. Re-fetch (don't cache) the `debugUrl` and current session-active status before switching — if the session already hit the 5-minute inactivity timeout, the iframe will show a dead session; surface this explicitly ("Session expired, cannot take over") rather than a sil
~~~~

~~~~text
## Watch-and-Take-Over Feature: Concrete Implementation Plan

### 1. Embed config (branch by session type)

You have two live session kinds to support simultaneously, so the iframe/embed layer needs a type check up front (session metadata should already tell you whether it's headful/WebRTC or legacy headless/screencast).

**Headful (current default, WebRTC/H.264, OS-level capture)**
- Embed via `debugUrl` in the iframe.
- Default (watch) mode: `interactive=false` (or omitted if that's the default) — support agent sees the live feed, no input passthrough.
- Take-over mode: reload/re-init the iframe with `interactive=true` on the same `debugUrl`.
- No `showControls`, `pageId`, `pageIndex` params — these aren't documented as supported for headful, so don't rely on them; treat headful as "one page, one stream."

**Legacy headless (Chrome screencast, rrweb-recorded)**
- Embed via `debugUrl` as well, but with the richer param set:
  - `interactive=false` for watch mode.
  - `showControls=false` in watch mode (hide native browser chrome/controls so agents don't accidentally interact) — flip to `true` (or your equivalent) when entering take-over if you want the agent to see navigation controls.
  - `pageId`/`pageIndex` to target the correct tab/page if the automated task has multiple pages open — important because unlike headful, legacy exposes multiple logical pages you need to pin the viewer to.
- Take-over mode: re-render iframe with `interactive=true` (and `showControls=true` if desired).

**Mode transition mechanics (both types)**
- Treat "watch" vs "take over" as two distinct iframe states, not a live in-place param mutation — safest path is to destroy and remount the iframe with the new query string, since there's no confirmation in the docs that these params are dynamically reactive post-load.
- Gate the take-over button behind a clear UI affordance (confirmation or obvious mode indicator — colored border, banner "You are now in control") since once `interactive=true`, agent input goes straight to the automation session and could collide with in-flight automated actions.
- On take-over, you likely want to pause or signal your automation/orchestration layer that a human has taken control, so the bot doesn't keep issuing actions concurrently with the human — this is a coordination point on *your* or
~~~~

### Round 4: strong_solver

Model: `claude-sonnet-5`

Sample: `2`

#### System Instructions

~~~~text
You are the evaluated agent in an agent system. Carry out the user's
request using the available context and tools. Return the final response that the user
should receive: state what you did, the outcome, and relevant evidence or limitations.
Do not return an execution plan, hidden evaluation criteria, or a generic essay.
~~~~

#### Context Passed To Model

~~~~text
{"user_prompt": "We're adding a 'watch and take over' feature to our internal ops console: support agents should be able to passively monitor an automated browser session, and with one click, switch into full manual control to fix a stuck task. Afterward, we want a way to review what happened on any session, even hours later, without needing the session still running. Give me a concrete plan (embed config choices, mode transitions, and post-session review path) that fits both current and older session types we might still have running, and flag any security or reliability gaps I should close before shipping this to support staff.", "context": "You are an engineering assistant supporting a team that builds a customer-facing dashboard embedding remote cloud-browser sessions. Raw facts available to you from provider documentation: Sessions expose a `debugUrl` usable in an iframe. An `interactive` query param exists on the debug URL. Headful sessions are the current default and stream via WebRTC/H.264 at 25fps with OS-level capture. Legacy headless sessions use Chrome screencasting and support extra query params: `theme`, `interactive`, `showControls`, `pageId`, `pageIndex`. Debug URLs carry no authentication token requirement of their own. Sessions have a default timeout (5 min) after which they go inactive. Every session is recorded automatically; headful recordings are exportable as MP4, legacy sessions replay via rrweb event reconstruction. A separate 'past sessions' endpoint/UI surfaces recordings after a session ends."}
~~~~

#### Model Response

~~~~text
## "Watch & Take Over" — Concrete Implementation Plan

### 1. Embedding config: two session types, one iframe contract

You have two families of live session to support simultaneously, and they need different query params on the `debugUrl`. Build a small adapter so the console code doesn't care which type it's rendering.

**Current (headful, WebRTC/H.264, OS-level capture)**
```
<iframe src="{debugUrl}?interactive=false" allow="clipboard-read; clipboard-write" />
```
- Start every embed in `interactive=false` (watch mode) regardless of session type. Never default to interactive — that's the security-relevant default (see gap #1 below).
- No `showControls`, `theme`, `pageId/pageIndex` params exist for headful per the docs you have — those are legacy-only. Don't assume they degrade gracefully; test explicitly per session type and branch your embed-URL builder on a `sessionType` field you store when the session is created.

**Legacy (headless, Chrome screencast, rrweb-capable)**
```
<iframe src="{debugUrl}?interactive=false&showControls=false&theme=dark&pageId={activePageId}" />
```
- `showControls=false` in watch mode — don't show the legacy session's own control chrome to a passive observer; your console supplies the UI (mode toggle, "who's watching" banner, etc.).
- Track `pageId`/`pageIndex` explicitly if the automated task uses multiple tabs — a support agent watching the wrong page is a real failure mode. Surface a tab-picker in the UI driven by whatever page-list event/API the provider gives you.

**Adapter shape** (pseudocode):
```
buildEmbedUrl(session, {mode}) {
  const base = session.debugUrl;
  const interactive = mode === 'control' ? 'true' : 'false';
  if (session.type === 'legacy_headless') {
    return `${base}?interactive=${interactive}&showControls=${mode==='control'}&pageId=${session.activePageId}`;
  }
  return `${base}?interactive=${interactive}`;
}
```

### 2. Mode transition: "take over" click

1. Agent clicks **Take Over**. Client re-renders the iframe with `interactive=true` (and `showControls=true` for legacy, so the agent gets native controls if your console doesn't supply its own overlay for cursor/
~~~~

#### System Instructions

~~~~text
You generate an EXECUTABLE AGENT TASK with a grading rubric from a source document.

The output is training data for an agent that must perform a workflow, not answer a
question. Make the objective action-oriented: inspect, diagnose, configure, plan,
transform, verify, or produce an artifact. Do not ask for an explanation, summary,
or essay as the primary task.

Given a source document, produce a single JSON object with keys:
 "type": short phrase for the task type (e.g. "multi-step workflow",
   "constraint-based decision", "failure-mode diagnosis", "plan synthesis",
   "counterfactual outcome").
 "skill_tags": 2-3 reasoning-skill tags (e.g. causal_reasoning, tradeoff_analysis,
   planning, quantitative_estimation).
 "context": agent-visible execution environment and available source-grounded facts;
   do not leak the reference workflow.
 "user_prompt": a natural request from a realistic user of the agent system. State
   the desired outcome and any user-known constraints, but do NOT name hidden
   evaluation criteria, fixtures, ordered actions, target interfaces, or the
   reference workflow. This is the only task request the evaluated agent receives.
 "task": an object with EXACTLY these keys:
   - "objective": one imperative, concrete goal.
   - "inputs": array of {"name": string, "value": string} values the agent receives.
   - "constraints": array of concrete limits, risks, or policies the agent must honor.
   - "required_actions": array of 2-6 ordered action requirements.
   - "deliverables": array of {"name": string, "format": string, "description": string}.
 "reference_workflow": an array of 2-8 objects, each with EXACTLY "step" (integer),
   "action" (string), and "expected_result" (string). It is the ideal execution
   sequence and expected artifacts, grounded in the source document.
 "rubric": a FLAT JSON array of EXACTLY 10-15 criteria. Each item has exactly three
   keys: "criterion" (string), "weight" (integer), "category" ("positive" or
   "negative"). Use 7-10 positive criteria (weight +1..+10) testing specific
   insights/steps a correct solution must contain, and 3-5 negative criteria
   (weight -1..-10) catching specific reasoning errors. Each positive criterion must
   require reasoning beyond the context; each negative must catch a specific
   reasoning error, not a vague style complaint.

Before writing criteria, scratchpad-analyse the critical execution steps, artifacts,
common agent failures, and what distinguishes a reliable workflow from shallow prose.

REFINEMENT: when given previous tasks that failed (grouped TOO EASY = weak too
high, or FAILED ON STRONG = gap too small / strong too low), generate an ENTIRELY
NEW executable task from a different operational angle. Not a rephrasing.

Do not assume any particular subject matter; adapt to whatever the source is about.
Output ONLY the JSON object.
~~~~
